data.name: David Ospina
data.description: Understudy to Vargas after the best part of a decade as first-choice. When he was little, during trips to the supermarket, Ospina would abandon his parents in search of the footballs. To begin with, he didn’t want to be a goalkeeper. He had all the makings of a striker: he could shoot with both feet and had an eye for goal. However, when the keeper in the Alexis García academy team didn’t turn up one day, Ospina put his hand up and went between the sticks. At 18, he was winning league titles as Atlético Nacional’s first-choice keeper. His international debut came almost by accident when Miguel Calero was sent off in a match against Uruguay and Ospina was thrown on in his place. More than 125 caps later, the 38-year-old arrives at his third World Cup. He has two FA Cups with Arsenal, playing the 2017 final against Chelsea, and a Coppa Italia with Napoli to his name.

data.name: Daniel Muñoz
data.description: Born in Amalfi, home of some of the biggest gold mines in Colombia, Muñoz was a late bloomer. He was a massive fan of Atlético Nacional and often went to watch their games. At 18, having been rejected by various clubs, Muñoz turned his back on football. He wanted to go to the United States to find a job but he couldn’t get a visa. Finally, the Total Soccer team opened their doors to him. “It wasn’t easy to come into a place and play with kids younger than you and knowing that some of your teammates were already professional,” Muñoz said. At the age of 21, he went pro with Águilas Doradas in Rionegro before attracting the interest of the club of his dreams, Atlético Nacional. From there he left for Genk in Belgium and was a major part of the Crystal Palace side that won the club’s first major trophy, the FA Cup, last year.

data.name: Jhon Lucumí
data.description: Destined to become a footballer. The day he was born, Colombia were knocked out of the 1998 World Cup by England, bringing an end to the Carlos Valderrama generation. It was at the club that Valderrama left for Europe, Deportivo Cali, where Lucumí started out. Ths scout Michel Arango saw him playing on a pitch in Cali’s San Luisito neighbourhood; he was only 10, playing as a striker, but stood out because of his height. Arango suggested he would be better off as a defender. He was playing for Cali’s first team at 17, making his debut as a left-back because the coach, Fernando Castro, was without one. Coached by the former Colombia centre-back Mario Yepes, Lucumí secured a move to Europe with Genk and is part of the Bologna team that reached the Champions League proper and won last year’s Coppa Italia.

data.name: Santiago Arias
data.description: The former PSV Eindhoven and Atlético Madrid full-back almost chose to pursue a different sport to football. “At school we had to do a sporting activity. I was ready to pick taekwondo because I really liked the karate films, but I chose football in the end,” Arias once told the magazine Bocas. It was his father, Raúl, who persuaded him. “He was a big football fan,” Arias said. “He was an Atlético Nacional supporter. We would go to the ground together. Everything was about football with him, 24/7. It’s thanks to him that I am where I am.” It wasn’t at Nacional where he started his career but La Equidad, via the Alexis García academy. García was manager of the first team and handed Arias his debut. Raúl never saw his son play for Colombia – he was tragically shot dead in 2009 when shielding children from armed robbers outside a school.

data.name: Kevin Castaño
data.description: Castaño’s parents split up shortly after he was born and his mother left their home town, Itagüí, for work. His father, Richard, raised him alongside his grandmother, María Noelia. Richard was also Kevin’s first coach – he was hired as a youth coach by Itagüí Ditaires (now Águilas Doradas) in 2008. “Kevin was football mad,” Richard told the newspaper El Tiempo. “He played local tournaments and caught the eye. I put him up front and he always scored goals. He had a good strike on him and was a great leader.” When Kevin made his first-team debut for Águilas in 2020, his father was in surgery after being admitted to hospital with Covid. After his operation the first thing Richard asked for was to watch the game back. Castaño received his first international call-up in 2023 and, after brief spells in Mexico and Russia, 25-year-old is now at River Plate.

data.name: Richard Ríos
data.description: A key cog in Néstor Lorenzo’s midfield, he once dreamed of being a futsal star but fate had other ideas. Started playing football on the streets of Vegachí, in the Antioquía region not too far from Medellín. The region is known for its gold mines but also for producing sugar cane and sweet treats containing guava fruit. “Football was a good alternative to heading down the wrong path,” Ríos once said. He caught the eye playing for Colombia at a South American under-18 futsal championship in Brazil and was given a trial at Flamengo. “Leaving your country is not easy for anyone but I was motivated by that. Those of us from outside Brazil see it as the country of football,” he said. That mixture of Antioquían grit and Brazilian magic helped him recover from a serious knee injury while playing on loan in Mexico and go on to land a move to Benfica after impressing at the Club World Cup last summer.

data.name: Luis Díaz
data.description: Anyone who watches the strong and combative Díaz play for Bayern would not think that he was once placed on a special diet in order to put on 10kg. That was at Barranquilla, a feeder club for Atlético Junior, where he came recommended by the legendary Carlos Valderrama. "El Pibe" spotted him playing for Colombia at a Copa América for indigenous communities in 2015, where John Jairo Díaz was the coach. “Valderrama was practically my boss,” the coach said. “He would say: ‘That skinny kid is going to play, he’ll make it as a pro.’ And look what happened.” Díaz’s father, Mane, was another key person in his career. Mane’s kidnapping by armed guerrillas on the Colombian-Venezuelan border, and safe return, made headline news around the world in 2023 when Díaz was playing for Liverpool, where he won the Premier League, an FA Cup and two League Cups.

data.name: Jorge Carrascal
data.description: In just eight months at Flamengo, Carrascal has won the Copa Libertadores, Brazil’s Serie A and the Carioca state championship. However, his career was almost over before it began. When he turned 18 he moved to Sevilla, who signed him despite a serious knee injury. He had surgery and was able to return for Sevilla’s B team but then the knee went again. During the process of a second operation he got a bacterial infection and was in danger of having the leg amputated. Fortunately he managed to recover. After his debut in Colombian football his Millonarios coach, Ricardo Lunari, compared him to Juan Román Riquelme. When he signed for River Plate, they called him the new Neymar. Carrascal didn’t like the comparisons. “Every player has their own career and wants to make a name for themself,” he told Olé. “Let’s hope one day a player will be nicknamed Carrascal. You never know!”

data.name: Jhon Córdoba
data.description: There are a few father-and-son duos that have played for Colombia – but only one where both have found the net for the Selección. Córdoba inherited his eye for goal from his father, Manuel Acisclo, who scored three times in 15 appearances in the 1980s. El Triciclo (the Tricycle), as he was known, was not just his mentor and biggest supporter but also a critical voice. “Jhon is a very strong person,” Manuel told Caracol Deportes. “I don’t like to talk to him before games. We have a chat afterwards but I tend to steer clear of football. The next day, when he’s calmed down, we’ll talk. If he’s made a mistake, I tell him: ‘You did this badly and this is why.’” Manuel is very demanding and used to keep tallies. “I scored 153 goals. He’s got a long way to go,” he would say. After spells in Mexico, Spain and Germany, 32-year-old Jhon has already passed that figure.

data.name: James Rodríguez
data.description: Not the first member of his family to play at a World Cup: his father was part of the Colombia squad at the under-20 edition in 1985. After Wilson James and Pilar Rubio split up, it was Rodríguez’s stepfather, Juan Carlos Restrepo, who encouraged him to follow his dream. He came through at Envigado, one of the best academies in Colombia. “Juan Carlos is an obsessive; he took it upon himself to make James a star. He deserves a lot of credit,” said Silvio Sandri, Rodríguez’s first agent, in 2014, the year he won the Golden Boot at the World Cup in Brazil and sealed a blockbuster move to Real Madrid. The two-time Champions League winner is now playing in MLS at 35 after some globetrotting that took in Merseyside, Qatar, Greece, Brazil and Mexico. This will be his third World Cup.

data.name: Jhon Arias
data.description: Quibdó is, perhaps, the poorest capital city of Colombia’s 33 departamentos and that is where Arias’s journey towards professional football began. It wasn’t easy – and he had to move abroad to get a chance to shine. “I’m from a beautiful place, where there aren’t many opportunities; that’s where this kid with dreams was born,” he told El Colombiano. “All those experiences and difficulties that I had as a kid have given me the certainty that all that effort was worth it.” From Quibdó, Arias went to Cali and then to youth sides in Mexico. After being rejected by Tijuana he went through four clubs back in Colombia before his performances for Santa Fe in the 2021 Copa Libertadores earned him a move to Fluminense, where he won the continental title in 2023. After a spell in the Premier League with Wolves, the 28-year-old is back in Brazil with Palmeiras.

data.name: Camilo Vargas
data.description: Knows a thing or two about ending curses, having done so at two of South America’s most traditional clubs. In 2012 he helped his first team, Santa Fe, win their first league title since 1975. And that had a lot to do with his mother-in-law, Sandra Merino. She didn’t like football but started to take an interest when her daughter, Ángela, got together with Vargas. A devout follower of Christ, Sandra told the Santa Fe directors that the team was being affected by witchcraft. Despite initial scepticism the club invited Sandra to meet the team and she cleaned their feet. After that the players took to the field barefoot before matches. It worked. When playing in Mexico in 2021, Vargas helped Atlas win their first title in 70 years. The shy goalkeeper rarely has much to say in front of the media and has the patience of a saint: he replaced David Ospina as Colombia’s first-choice only in his late 30s.

data.name: Yerry Mina
data.description: Despite his lankiness as a youngster, Mina wasn’t very good at heading the ball – something he would make up for later in life. He had trials at Deportivo Cali but didn’t make it. He also played for América de Cali, when he used to hitch a ride on lorries to save money on bus fares. He ended up at Deportivo Pasto, where two of his teammates, Marlon Piedrahíta and Camilo Pérez, started to urge him to make the most of his lofty stature. By the time he signed for Santa Fe he was a major aerial presence. He played a key role in the Copa Sudamericana triumph in 2015 and earned a move to Palmeiras before a big break at Barcelona. He was Colombia’s top scorer at the 2018 World Cup, each of his three goals coming with his head, including a late equaliser against England in the last 16. After a five-year spell at Everton, the 31-year-old has become a favourite in Sardinia.

data.name: Gustavo Puerta
data.description: There are five professional football clubs in the departamento of Valle where Puerta was born, but he couldn’t force his way into any of them – despite impressing at football schools in the area. He sought his fortune in the capital with second-tier Bogotá FC, from where he made the massive jump to Europe with Bayer Leverkusen. Xabi Alonso was there waiting for him. “It’s a privilege to be around people of his ilk and this level of players. He was one of the best in his position. He has taught me a lot – he helps you explore your potential,” Puerta said. After a spell in the Championship with Hull, he has found a home in northern Spain with Racing, this past season helping them back to La Liga after 14 years away. The former Colombia Under-20s captain scored on his first senior cap against New Zealand last November.

data.name: Juan Portilla
data.description: Portilla’s first steps in football were adventurous. His father, an electrician by profession, signed him up to a small football school in Cali but couldn’t afford to keep him there. Shortly after, one of his father’s friends recommended him to Boca Juniors de Cali. His senior debut came for Universitario de Popayán, owned by the same people as Boca, but Portilla wanted to expand his horizons. He tried out for a third-tier side in Jacksonville in the United States and then again for a team in Chile’s second division, Melipilla. His love for his family brought him back to Colombia, where he eventually broke through at América, the local rivals of the team he supported as a boy, Deportivo Cali. After a spell at Talleres in Argentina, he moved to Brazil last January.

data.name: Jefferson Lerma
data.description: It took the Crystal Palace midfielder a long time to gain the trust of his coaches as a youngster. After being rejected by three clubs, he ended up at Atlético Huila. Fed up at his lack of opportunities, Lerma went back to his home town, El Cerrito, across the other side of the Andes. When Álvaro de Jesús Gómez came in as coach of Huila in 2013, he asked which of the under-20s could make the step up to the first team. When he was told about Lerma, he went in search of him and told him to come back, even paying his travel expenses. Soon he was a starter for Huila, two years later he was in Europe and, in 2016, he played for Colombia at the Olympics. His inclusion in the 2018 World Cup squad was controversial because he had not played a part in qualifying. Lerma almost quit the Selección in face of the criticism but he was convinced to stay on. He earned a move to Bournemouth that summer and is now an integral part of the national squad. Won the FA Cup with Palace in 2024-25.

data.name: Johan Mojica
data.description: Has played in Europe for almost 12 years now and his physical attributes have allowed him to thrive in Spain and Italy. He wasn’t always blessed with such a physique. His first club, Academia Compensar, in Bogotá doubted that he could make it in the game. “When Johan arrived, he was underweight for his age … we couldn’t see how that boy was going to improve,” Jorge Caracol Serna, a coach at Academia and the future manager of Colombia’s under-15s and under-17s, told Gol. “But his technique and ability to read the play were so good that we gave him some time.” They made him a long-term plan to help him gain weight and gave him a monthly alimentary supplement. “The only thing that grew was his hair,” joked Serna. Now 33, Mojica has played for a host of clubs in La Liga as well as Atalanta in Serie A.

data.name: Willer Ditta
data.description: In qualifying Ditta played only two games, starting once, and watched another three from the bench. He felt he needed to be doing more to earn a spot on the plane to North America, so he decided to train in his own time. “It’s for me to keep improving on a personal and sporting level. We decided that we would work hard in these two months before the World Cup to improve in a physical and technical aspect,” he told the journalist Adrián Esparza Oteo. Born in Ibirico, Ditta grew up in Barranquilla in the north before moving to La Jagua de Ibirico with his mother. There weren’t many opportunities to play football, he was rejected by the regional side and ended up playing “for fizzy drinks” outside Valledupar’s stadium. Pablo Zuleta, the coach who had initially discarded him, saw him playing in a school tournament and realised his mistake. After a spell in Argentina, he has established himself in Mexico.

data.name: Cucho Hernández
data.description: Earned the nickname Cucho by chance. He once turned up to a game in his home town Pereira with his head shaved and his friends starting calling him Cuchu after the bald former Inter and Real Madrid midfielder Esteban Cambiasso. That eventually turned into Cucho. Football is in his blood: his parents, Néstor and Yanet, met while playing a tournament and fell in love straight away. From a young age he joined his parents at tournaments and started to play himself, eventually turning out for the Risaralda regional team. That was where Hernán Lisi, the coach of Deportivo Pereira, spotted him. “I want that player!” he shouted when he saw him perform. After scoring twice on his debut for Colombia, the 26-year-old has struggled to establish himself.

data.name: Juan Fernando Quintero
data.description: Only two players have scored goals for Colombia in more than one World Cup. One is Juan Cuadrado and the other is "Juanfer", who, having scored against Ivory Coast in 2014, snuck a free-kick under the wall against Japan four years later. Quintero is known for coming up with the spectacular. He won goal of the tournament for his strike against El Salvador at the 2013 Under-20 World Cup and an extra-time stunner helped deliver River Plate the 2018 Copa Libertadores title against their superclásico rivals, Boca Juniors. But away from football, Quintero has had to live with personal tragedy. His father went missing in 1993, when he was two years old. “I have the right to know what happened to my father and that is what I want to know because I’ve suffered and I’ve seen my family go through it,” Quintero said in 2020, when Eduardo Zapateiro, who was investigated as part of his father’s case (the pair had an argument shortly before Quintero’s father’s disappearance), was named commander general of the Colombian army.

data.name: Jaminton Campaz
data.description: Cheated death to become a footballer. As a kid in Chontal near the southern border with Ecuador, he made money from fishing and one day was bitten by a snake. One of his colleagues squeezed the wound and stopped things from getting worse. Campaz comes from a family of footballers. His mother, Betzabet, was a goalkeeper; his brother, Mike, played for Equatorial Guinea and his cousin, Carlos Darwin Quintero, played for Colombia in 2014 World Cup qualifying and had a long career in Mexico and MLS – he still plays for Millonarios. Campaz fought for a career in his own right, trying out at Deportivo Pasto, but his mother forgot to sign a document and he was forced to leave. He also failed a trial at América de Cali. It was Mike who took him to Tolima, where he started his journey as a pro.

data.name: Deiver Machado
data.description: Machado has come a long way since taking his first steps in football on a 14m-long pitch built by the local community in the small town of Tadó in Colombia’s Chocó departamento. His family were displaced by violence and went to live in Santa Cecilia, over the border in the Risaralda departamento, and that’s where a nine-year-old Machado had tryouts at Deportivo Pereira. Machado had to wait to make his first-team debut after the club’s future was plunged into doubt by relegation. One of his brothers suggested he go to Medellín to have a trial at Atlético Nacional. The 32-year-old has found a home in France, where he has played for Toulouse, Lens and now Nantes. At the start of May, he said his main aim was to go to the World Cup. By the end of the month, he was in the squad.

data.name: Davinson Sánchez
data.description: It can be tricky for South American players who are not from Brazil or Argentina to seal a move to Europe, let alone be greeted with great fanfare. “Who the hell is Davinson Sánchez?” asked one Tottenham fan online when the defender signed from Ajax in 2017. By then he had already won a Copa Libertadores medal with Atlético Nacional and enjoyed a bright start in the Eredivisie. He was Spurs’ record signing at £42m but had to win over his doubters before becoming a key part of the team that reached the 2019 Champions League final. But what if he hadn’t become a footballer? “I’m good at maths, so I would have studied something to do with numbers. I don’t know, something like engineering or administration,” he told the magazine Bocas in 2018, the year he made his World Cup debut. Now a two-time Turkish Super Lig champion with Galatasaray.

data.name: Álvaro Montero
data.description: If anything marks out Montero it’s his enormous self-confidence. When he arrived at Vélez Sarsfield last year, he said: “I’m a goalkeeper who is very difficult to score against – that’s my best quality. After that, we have ups and downs, strengths and weaknesses.” Montero tried his luck abroad with São Caetano in Brazil and San Lorenzo in Argentina but barely played. It wasn’t until he was back in Colombia with Tolima that he started to gain confidence. They had only won one league title in 63 years but with Montero in goal they won two in four years, shocking Atlético Nacional and Millonarios in second legs away from home. After the 2021 final Montero celebrated by giving the referee a big but unrequited hug from behind, pulling up his shorts. When the Tolima coach, Alberto Gamero, went to Millonarios, Montero told him: “Boss, if you want to win trophies there, you’ll have to take me with you.” He did and they won three of them.

data.name: Luis Suárez
data.description: Not that one. However, the former Granada striker has had an outstanding first season in Portugal and some of Europe’s biggest clubs are looking at him. But this time last year the picture was very different. Yes, he was scoring goals in Spain’s second tier with Almería, but he wasn’t really in the frame for the national team. What’s more, he was coming off the back of an injury that had affected his mental health. “I went through depression, which I’m still coming out of bit by bit with some work and help from psychologists and people close to me. I’d hit rock bottom,” he told Marca. The support of his partner, Carolina, has been key. After scoring almost a goal a game in the Primeira Liga this season, the 28-year-old is now a permanent fixture for Colombia.

data.name: Andrés Gómez
data.description: Has picked up his fair share of nicknames during his short career. When he first came through at Millonarios they called him Diablito (the Little Devil) because of his mischievous style of play. He’s also been called Yanfri after a kid that went viral on social media who apparently looked like him. One nickname he doesn’t like is Tinito – his style of play is similar to Tino Asprilla – because Asprilla was an Atlético Nacional man, playing for Millonarios’s big rivals. Gómez had a tough start to life. “As I kid, I grew up watching some of my friends get killed and saw others follow the wrong path. But my whole family has always been into football,” he wrote in Chocó 7 Días. He also admitted that he himself had almost gone down the wrong path: he tried to rob someone once but got caught. The 24-year-old has two passions: motorbikes and dancing. “Wherever I go, I always do my neighbourhood's popular dance. It’s a way of expressing where I come from,” he said.
